Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered even though I put in a lot of hours. I'd usually listen to lecture recordings while doing other things around my apartment, since I learn better by hearing things. I tried to organize by course deadlines, but with five online courses and limited resources, I often felt like I was just trying to keep my head above water. Some weeks I'd study late into the night, other weeks I'd cram everything into a few days.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how I was feeling emotionally. I'd have these bursts of productivity where I'd knock out a bunch of assignments, then periods where I just couldn't bring myself to focus even when I knew I should. Deadlines definitely drove when I studied more than any set schedule. I always meant to be more consistent, but life and my anxiety made that really hard to maintain.
Q3: Usually it was assignment deadlines that got me to log in, or sometimes I'd get a notification about a discussion post. I logged in pretty frequently, like nineteen times or so, because I was always checking if I'd missed something important. My anxiety made me paranoid about missing deadlines, so even when I didn't feel like engaging, I'd still check the LMS. Sometimes my peer support group would remind me to check something specific.
Q4: Yeah, checking the LMS kind of became a nervous habit for me. I'd wake up and check it on my phone, even before getting out of bed sometimes. It wasn't really about being excited to learn—it was more about making sure I hadn't missed anything that would hurt my grade. The peer support intervention actually helped because my study buddies would message about logging in together, which made it feel less isolating.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I'm an auditory learner. I watched almost three hours worth of videos, and I'd often replay sections multiple times to really absorb the information. The discussion forums were helpful too when my peers would explain concepts differently than the professor. Having that peer interaction made the material feel more accessible when I was struggling to understand something on my own.
Q6: I tended to skip over the supplementary reading materials because I just couldn't afford all the textbooks and my internet isn't always reliable for accessing online resources. Some of the optional practice exercises I'd ignore because I was already overwhelmed with the required work across all five courses. If something didn't directly connect to an assignment or exam, it usually got pushed aside. I know that probably hurt my exam scores.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to passing the course or if my peers were also doing it. When my study group discussed something, it suddenly felt more relevant and less like busywork. I also valued activities where I could listen rather than just read, since that matches how I learn best. If something felt too abstract or disconnected from assignments, I'd lose interest quickly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford with my workload and limited resources. Required activities had this weight to them—I knew I had to complete them or face consequences, which my anxiety amplified into something almost unbearable. Optional stuff felt nice in theory, but when you're juggling five courses and feeling sad most of the time, you have to prioritize survival over enrichment. I wish I'd had more energy for the optional content.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone during my commute or while doing chores since I don't always have reliable internet. I borrowed textbooks from friends or found free resources online. I also recorded myself summarizing concepts and played those back—it sounds weird, but hearing my own voice explaining things helped me remember better.
Q10: There were many times when I was genuinely learning but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd have a lecture playing on repeat while cooking dinner, or I'd be discussing concepts with my peer support group over text messages. Sometimes I'd spend hours with a borrowed textbook working through problems by hand. The LMS only saw me when I logged in, but my actual learning happened in all these invisible spaces throughout my day.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video once, download it or take detailed notes while listening, and then never touch the LMS version again. My internet connection isn't always stable, so downloading things for offline use was essential. I'd listen to the same lecture recording three or four times over a week, but the system only saw that initial access. It was more practical for me to work offline given my situation.
Q12: There were definitely periods when engaging felt almost impossible. When my sadness got really heavy, even logging into the LMS felt like climbing a mountain. My anxiety would spike around exam time, which paradoxically made it harder to actually study effectively. Sometimes financial stress would consume my mental energy, leaving nothing for coursework. Those were the times I relied most heavily on my peer support group to keep me accountable.
Q13: All of those things affected me at different points. The workload from five courses was genuinely overwhelming, and I often felt confused by material but too anxious to ask questions publicly. Fatigue was constant because my anxiety disrupted my sleep. When I had work or family responsibilities competing for my attention, the LMS was usually what got sacrificed first, even though I'd still complete assignments at the last minute.
Q14: Oh, definitely. Sometimes I'd have a video playing but my mind would be completely elsewhere, lost in anxious thoughts or just feeling too sad to absorb anything. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. There were times I'd log in, stare at the screen for twenty minutes, and log out having accomplished nothing meaningful. The engagement was there on paper, but not in reality.
Q15: I postponed studying for exams more than I should have, even knowing how important they were. My anxiety would make me avoid things that felt overwhelming, which was counterproductive because the avoidance just made the anxiety worse. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, then tomorrow would come and I'd feel even more paralyzed. My assignment completion was high because those had clear deadlines, but open-ended exam prep was harder to force myself into.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to engage that I couldn't access before. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically in the days before things were due. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure of a deadline cut through my emotional fog in a way nothing else did. Without deadlines, I honestly don't know if I would have engaged at all during my lowest periods.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Good feedback would give me a brief boost, but it faded quickly. Critical feedback, even when constructive, would send my anxiety spiraling and sometimes made me avoid that course for a few days. I appreciated when instructors gave detailed comments because it helped me understand what to do differently. My peer support group helped me process feedback in healthier ways than I would have alone.
Q18: The course structure was a double-edged sword. Having clear modules and deadlines helped my anxious brain know what to expect. But the volume of content across five courses made it hard to engage deeply with any single one. I appreciated when courses had flexibility in how to demonstrate learning, since that accommodated my auditory learning style. Rigid structures sometimes clashed with my unpredictable emotional states.
Q19: Courses that offered audio or video content made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. Discussion forums where peers could help each other were valuable, especially with the peer support intervention. What made things harder was when courses required expensive materials I couldn't afford, or when participation required reliable internet access I didn't always have. Timed assessments were particularly difficult given my anxiety levels.
Q20: I think that could reflect someone who's trying really hard but struggling to produce quality work, maybe due to confusion or personal challenges. It might also be someone using the LMS as a form of anxious checking—logging in repeatedly without actually accomplishing much, which I definitely did sometimes. Or they could be engaging with content but not understanding it well enough to complete assignments. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My exam scores weren't great, but I passed, and a lot of my real learning happened offline where the LMS couldn't track it. Someone might download everything once and study intensively from those materials. Or they might have strong prior knowledge and not need as much LMS engagement. The system measures presence, not necessarily comprehension or effort that happens elsewhere.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone logs in, what they click on, and whether they submit work on time. They're good at tracking surface-level behaviors. But they miss the quality of attention, the emotional state behind the engagement, and all the learning that happens offline. They can't tell if someone is genuinely absorbing material or just going through motions. They definitely can't capture the support networks and conversations that actually help students learn.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing requirements. It's when you find yourself thinking about course concepts outside of study time, or when you genuinely want to discuss ideas with others. For me, engagement also means showing up even when it's hard—which I tried to do despite my sadness and anxiety. It's more than just logging in; it's being mentally and emotionally present with the learning.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my peer support group meant to my success in this course. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures while managing my daily life. It can't see that I was battling significant anxiety and sadness while still managing to complete nearly all my assignments. It misses the effort behind each login, the tears sometimes, and the small victories that felt huge given what I was dealing with internally.
